For all integers n, n^3 ≡ n (mod 2) — Fermat's little theorem for p=2.
For all integers n, n^2 ≡ n (mod 2), i.e., every integer squared equals itself mod 2.

claim: For all primes p, phi(p) | p^3 - p, i.e., (p-1) | p(p-1)(p+1), which is trivially true since p-1 appears as a factor.
For all integers n, n^3 - n = 6 * C(n+1, 3) for n >= 2, and C(n+1,3) is always a positive integer, so the sequence (n^3-n)/6 for n=2,3,4,... gives 1, 4, 10, 20, 35, ... (tetrahedral numbers).
For n >= 2, the tetrahedral number T_n = n(n+1)(n+2)/6, and (n^3-n)/6 = (n-1)n(n+1)/6 = T_{n-1}, the (n-1)th tetrahedral number.
For all integers n, the map n -> n^3 mod 6 is the identity: n^3 ≡ n (mod 6). The fixed points under cubing mod 6 are all residues 0,1,2,3,4,5.

For all n, vp(n*(n-1)*(n+1), 2) >= 1 and vp(n*(n-1)*(n+1), 3) >= 1, since among three consecutive integers one is divisible by 2 and one by 3.
For all nonzero n, vp(n*(n-1)*(n+1), 2) >= 1 means at least one of n, n-1, n+1 is even, which is obvious since consecutive integers alternate parity.
For all odd n, vp(n^3 - n, 2) = vp((n-1)*(n+1), 2) + vp(n, 2) = vp((n-1)(n+1), 2), and since n odd means n-1 and n+1 are consecutive evens, one of them is ≡ 0 mod 4, giving vp >= 3.
